explanation: This LTL property captures the requirement that the autopilot will transition from the MANEUVER state to the STANDBY state only when the pilot is in control (standby) and the sensor data is good. It does this by ensuring that if the pilot is in control and the sensor data is good, then the autopilot will eventually be in the STANDBY state. The formula (standby && good) -> F(state_is_STANDBY) ensures this. We need to add the condition pullup to the antecedent to ensure that we are actually in the MANEUVER state and the autopilot will transition to STANDBY as required by the input language.

This LTL formula captures the requirement that the autopilot should transition from the MANEUVER state to the STANDBY state when the pilot is in control (standby) and the sensor data is good. It uses the atomic propositions 'standby' and 'good' to represent the pilot being in control and the sensor data being good, respectively. It uses the atomic propositions 'state_is_MANEUVER' and 'state_is_STANDBY' to represent the autopilot being in the MANEUVER and STANDBY states, respectively. The formula ensures that whenever the pilot is in control and the sensor data is good, the autopilot will eventually transition from the MANEUVER state to the STANDBY state. It also indicates that the autopilot will never transition to the STANDBY state unless both conditions (pilot in control and good sensor data) are met.

This LTL property ensures that when the pilot is in control (standby) and sensor data is good, the autopilot will eventually transition from the MANEUVER state to the STANDBY state. The property uses the "until" operator to express this transition, ensuring that the autopilot remains in the MANEUVER state until the pilot is in control (standby) and sensor data is good, at which point the autopilot transitions to the STANDBY state.

This LTL property ensures that if the autopilot is in the MANEUVER state, and the pilot is in control (standby) and sensor data is good, the autopilot will eventually transition to the STANDBY state. This property uses the implication operator to express the condition of the pilot being in control and sensor data being good, and the "until" operator to express the transition to the STANDBY state. It does not guarantee that the autopilot will stay in the MANEUVER state until the condition is met.

This LTL property ensures that the autopilot will eventually transition from the MANEUVER state to the STANDBY state only when the pilot is in control (standby) and sensor data is good (good). The condition "standby" indicates the pilot is in control, and "good" ensures the sensor data is reliable. The property uses the "until" operator (U) to specify the transition from "state_is_MANEUVER" to "state_is_STANDBY" under the given conditions.

This LTL property captures the requirement that the autopilot should transition from the "MANEUVER" state to the "STANDBY" state whenever the pilot is in control (indicated by "standby" being true) and the sensor data is good (indicated by "good" being true). This is achieved by the "until" operator (U), which ensures that the autopilot remains in the "MANEUVER" state until both "standby" and "good" become true. Once these conditions are met, the autopilot is required to transition to the "STANDBY" state, indicated by "state_is_STANDBY" becoming true.

This LTL property captures the requirement that the autopilot must transition from the MANEUVER state to the STANDBY state when both the pilot is in control (standby) and the sensor data is good.  The property uses the "until" (U) operator to indicate that the autopilot must eventually transition to the STANDBY state while the pilot is in control (standby) and the sensor data is good.  The property also uses the "next" (X) operator to ensure that the transition happens in the next time step.

This LTL property captures the requirement that whenever the autopilot is in the MANEUVER state, it will eventually transition to the STANDBY state as long as the pilot is in control (standby) and the sensor data is good.  The property uses the "until" (U) operator to indicate that the autopilot must eventually transition to the STANDBY state while the pilot is in control (standby) and the sensor data is good.  The property also uses the "globally" (G) operator to ensure that this behavior holds true for all time steps.
output_LTL: G(state_is_MANEUVER -> F(standby & good) U X state_is_STANDBY)

explanation: This LTL property captures the requirement that whenever the autopilot is in the MANEUVER state, it will always transition to the STANDBY state as long as the pilot is in control (standby) and the sensor data is good.  The property uses the "releases" (V) operator to indicate that the autopilot must transition to the STANDBY state while the pilot is in control (standby) and the sensor data is good.  The property also uses the "globally" (G) operator to ensure that this behavior holds true for all time steps.  It is more strict than the previous version. It is possible that the previous version will be satisfied if the system is already in standby state. This version of the requirement is more demanding and ensures that when in MANEUVER state it will always transition to STANDBY if both conditions are true.

This LTL property captures the requirement that the autopilot must transition from the MANEUVER state to the STANDBY state when the pilot is in control (standby) and sensor data is good.  The property uses the "until" (U) operator to indicate that the autopilot must eventually transition to the STANDBY state while the pilot is in control (standby) and the sensor data is good.  The property also uses the "next" (X) operator to ensure that the transition happens in the next time step.  The previous versions were more strict. This version of the requirement is more lenient and allows the system to remain in the MANEUVER state until the conditions become satisfied.
output_LTL: G(state_is_MANEUVER -> F(standby & good) U X state_is_STANDBY)

The LTL formula states that whenever the autopilot is in the MANEUVER state ('pullup' is true), if the pilot is in control ('standby' is true) and the sensor data is good ('good' is true), then eventually the autopilot will transition to the STANDBY state ('state_is_STANDBY' will be true). The formula does not require the autopilot to transition to STANDBY immediately. It only requires that the transition will eventually occur.
